author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text:
Re: 'Stick'-welding (MMA). Please don't take offense, I love the channel. But, please, let go of the 'bleep'  rod!  AND for the love of God go on just a 30-60 minute welding course. It's painful to watch (both of you), you are acquiring habits that will seriously frustrate you later, and could really bite you in the arse one day. There has to be a local welder who would take you though the proper basics, maybe even do a brief turn on camera for/with you.
Basically you need to cover the following;

1) 'parallel butt joint' (same and differing thickness)
2) internal corner joint (same and differing thickness)
3) external corner joint (same and differing thickness)
4) butted 'T' joint (same and differing thickness)
And of the above;
5) single/multiple-pass welds (VERY important)
6) single/both sides passes
7) one-side full-penetration welds

Also try 'vertical' joint welding (you'll learn a lot from just 10-mins of trying that).

Sorry to sound so critical, I mean well.
Peace and goodwill.
Thanks for sharing your adventures.

author: @timothygorman7528
text: Hey Luke.  I feel like you might get some criticisms on this one, but don't let it discourage you or slow you down.  There's a lot to be said for being "self taught" for the most part when it comes to anything, and it's true, there is always more than one way to skin a cat.   You'll be going to school soon, I think, if I remember correctly from a previous video, for engineering.  I went to school for Flight, but chose a 4 year university program which included two years of Aircraft Maintenance, and the last place I thought I'd find myself was in a welding class.  I quite enjoyed it really, and there's the potential for a lot of welding in aviation.  It's been 30 years since I have welded anything except plumbing fixtures (which is completely different), and I don't remember much concerning the various methods; I probably remember the most about oxyacetylene though.  With aviation, we're usually working with aluminum, or steel tubing.  Welding tubing is great fun (said no one.)  In this stage of the game for you, in the kinds of things you're doing with Rob, the goal is the end-goal - two pieces of metal stuck together.  However, there's so much to learn in proper technique's, setups, what kind of weld is actually appropriate, and most importantly, that the weld does what it's supposed to do.  With aircraft frames, this is extremely important with exposure to the elements (corrosion concerns), the vibrations of the motor, stresses of forces experienced in flight, etc - the welds have to be perfect; not so much for tooling around on a homebuilt scooter.  The point is the level of knowledge and experience you're currently at is just fine for the kinds of things you're doing.  When you get to doing welding in school, you're going to learn so much more, and there will be challenges completing all of the "exercises" in technique which may differ from the way you taught yourself.  You have one of the most outgoing, excited and creative minds I've seen, and I love watching your ideas come to fruition.  I can't wait to see what you do with the knowledge and training an engineering program will give you.

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text:
Welding Tube IS fun!
Back in the day I learned to Gas Weld ...... Mainly 4" to 2" Tee's. Welding one of those where you cant just flip it over is a learning experience! To my dying day I will remember what having a weld puddle directly over my head is like! ;o) If nothing else it teaches good sphincter control. 
One of the things they did on a regular basis was "Destructive Testing" where you would cut a strip at right angles through your weld and polish it to a mirror finish and get the glass out for a good look at what you could do. They would then put the strip in a vice and hammer it until the bend went the opposite direction it started. One piece was a pass, two a fail!
Maybe you and Rob should each do a couple of welds and do a destructive test ..... just in the cause of friendly competition ;o) It might be educational too ;o)

replies.author: @timothygorman7528
replies.text: @totherarf I like the idea of a flat out "welding" competition :)

replies.author: @timothygorman7528
replies.text: @totherarf I still have a lump of scar, dead center, palm side of my right middle finger from an unfortunate incident during oxyacetylene welding of tubing.  I was on a stool, torch in right hand, rod in left and had a rather juicy "POP" - a piece of molten metal made it to the stool and burned through my pants and well, "got me" front and center, lol.  I jumped back, knocking the stool over, had a quick chuckle at the whole thing and went to upright the stool, but I'd managed somehow to "bend" the rod and the end of it wasn't where I was expecting it to be, and burned a 1/8" deep hole into the backside of that knuckle :P

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: @timothygorman7528 Yeah! To be fair I always had the gloves on when the flame was! ...... Went through some Jeans though ;o)

replies.author: @timothygorman7528
replies.text: @totherarf I must have turned off the torch gasses and laid it down and taken the glove off to right the stool?  I can't imagine me welding without gloves either; but I have the burn to prove I did this somehow though, lol.  Another short but funny welding story is that when I was grinding a stub of tubing to weld it, I lost it around the grinding wheel and it lodged in my finger, rode down the surface of the nail and penetrated a good third of an inch beneath the skin of my cuticle without breaking the skin.. hurt like hell, only bled a little; but a minute or two later we all got called over to the center table to watch a demonstration by the instructor; I started getting dizzy and ended leaving the lab and collapsing into a lounge chair just outside the doors - ha ha... I don't do well with blood.  I don't get sick, but either I handle it or I don't, and when I don't, I'm out like a light, lol.

author: @WileHeCoyote
text: Great video LUKE! AND GREAT WELDS MATE! now that you have arc welding down, all the rest are a breeze by comparison...... except maybe TIG aluminum welding  lol

replies.author: @timothygorman7528
replies.text: Yeah, I think it was TIG welding I looked forward to the most and hated the most; For training purposes, University had metal dividers along a table as work stations - I was constantly grounding out on the dividers while trying to TIG weld, and it would be one zap with 6 burns, lol - one across each finger, the thumb and my palm, ha ha ha ha ha.
